sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks (IT Delivery Manager)
SF Next Step confirms leadership change delayed project, now restarting
metrics.narrative: Sophie Onyeks articulated clear operational pain on the 22 Apr 2026 discovery call: manual contract management via Excel spreadsheets and SharePoint, no centralized intake process (requests arrive by email with no standardized form), no visibility into highest-risk suppliers, and inability to produce spend compliance reports quickly. Birmingham City Council's annual revenue is £1.7 billion, and the procurement team of ~65 people manages an estate with no unified view of active contracts, purchase orders, or supplier base. Sophie stated the team wants to 'get away from all of that and have a central system' and achieve 'best practice' processes. No quantified savings target, cycle-time reduction goal, or ROI case has been articulated yet — the buyer has not conducted an ROI workshop or stated a cost-per-manual-process metric. The business case is qualitative efficiency/compliance-driven at this stage; quantifying the value case is a recommended next move post-RFI.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks
SF Next Step: Sophie coordinating RFI primer support with procurement lead
champion.narrative: No champion has been identified. Sophie Onyeks (IT Delivery Manager, Influencer contact role) is the primary contact and the deal's operational quarterback ('I'm just running things... I'm a grunt worker... leading on the procurement and soft market testing, pre market engagement'), but she explicitly stated 'I'm not going to be one of the big people doing the evaluations.' She is facilitating the RFI process and coordinating with the procurement lead (referenced in Next Step as part of the approval chain), but she has no decision authority, no budget control, and no stated advocacy for Zycus over the 6 other Gartner vendors in the RFI. The 22 Apr call was transactional discovery (Anthony Gray asking qualification questions, Sophie answering); no evidence of Sophie pushing Zycus internally, introducing us to John Coyne or Lucy Moody, or sharing competitive intelligence. She is engaged and responsive (last contact 25 Jun 2026, 5 days ago) but neutral. Risk: with 7 vendors in the RFI and no champion, Zycus is competing on product/price alone with no internal advocate. Developing Sophie into a coach (if not a full champion, given her non-evaluator role) and identifying a champion within the evaluation committee (procurement or contract management evaluator with access to John Coyne/Lucy Moody) is the top priority post-RFI.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks and Anthony Gray
SF Next Step: no competitor named
competition.narrative: Intent is the incumbent procurement platform (contract expires August 2027), described by Sophie on the 22 Apr call as limited in contract management capabilities and not meeting the council's needs ('it does do some contract management, but nowhere near what we needed'). Intent is being displaced, so it is the do-nothing/status-quo competitor but not a live threat for the new award. Beyond Intent, Sophie stated the council has 'reached out to 7 suppliers' — all Gartner Magic Quadrant vendors — but has not named the other 6. Zycus is competing blind until post-RFI evaluation (6 Jul 2026 deadline). The competitive field will clarify after RFI responses are reviewed and the buyer selects vendors for workshops/demos. Anthony Gray noted on the call that Zycus recently achieved 'right hand side of the leader status in Gartner' and that the UK public sector 'is a market that's opened up for us' in the last 6 months, positioning Zycus as a credible Gartner-validated peer. No other competitor has been praised, mentioned, or compared. The wedge: Zycus's 20-year unified platform (not bolted-on acquisitions), agentic AI (Merlin intake control tower, autonomous negotiation for tail spend, auto-read contracts), and full Source-to-Pay depth including the contract management and spend visibility gaps Sophie described. Post-RFI, the competitive landscape will determine whether Zycus is up against SAP Ariba, Coupa, Ivalua, GEP, Jaggaer, or others; right now the field is unknown.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks
SF Next Step: leadership change delayed project, now restarting
identify_pain.narrative: Sophie Onyeks (IT Delivery Manager, pain owner) articulated specific operational pain on the 22 Apr 2026 call: (1) disparate systems (Intent for procurement, Oracle ERP for authorizing spend, but 'neither of them does what we need to do in terms of contract management especially, but also quite a few of the procurement steps'); (2) heavy manual processes ('the team are at the moment using a lot of Excel spreadsheets... for contract management especially, and using SharePoint for documents'); (3) no centralized intake ('there is no standard way of procurement coming into the procurement team at the moment... they could come via email, there isn't even... an Excel sheet that you fill out'); (4) no single view of risk ('if I asked you right now to give me a single view of your highest risk suppliers, how quickly could you produce that report?' — Sophie: 'that would be too difficult'); (5) no spend compliance visibility or reporting dashboard (most contract management is in Excel, no mention of Power BI or modern analytics). The council's £1.7 billion annual revenue and ~65-person procurement team scale the pain. Leadership approved the initiative; Lucy Moody (Assistant Director, Executive Sponsor) is the sponsor. Compelling event: Intent contract expires August 2027, forcing a decision. Pain is confirmed; the gap is quantifying the cost/impact of the current state (manual FTE burn, compliance risk exposure, tail-spend leakage) to build an ROI case.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks and Anthony Gray
SF Next Step: framework preference question added to RFI
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics (MSA/SOW, legal review, InfoSec, procurement channel) have not been discussed. The buyer is considering CCS framework, G-Cloud, or open market routes, and Zycus's CCS application status (expected approval June 2026, today is 30 Jun 2026) is a near-term paper-process dependency. If Zycus is not yet on CCS and the buyer selects that route, a GSI subcontract workaround (Capgemini/KPMG/Deloitte as prime on G-Cloud, Zycus as sub) is the fallback. Sophie confirmed on the 22 Apr call that Zycus partners (Deloitte, KPMG, Capgemini) could serve as prime contractor via G-Cloud with Zycus subcontracted, and Anthony Gray (Zycus VP EMEA) stated 'we have approval on Cyber Essentials here in The UK' (InfoSec baseline met). No discussion yet of MSA/SOW redline process, legal review timeline, or procurement authority thresholds. The August 2027 Intent contract expiry is the forcing function; working backwards, the buyer aims for contract award by end of 2026 or Q1 2027. Clarifying the commercial route and confirming Zycus CCS status (or GSI subcontract path) is the immediate next step.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks
SF Next Step: RFI timeline 6 Jul 2026 deadline, leadership change delay, Claire chasing for update
decision_process.narrative: The buyer is running a formal public-sector procurement: (1) RFI/pre-market engagement with 7 Gartner Magic Quadrant vendors (including Zycus), RFI deadline 6 Jul 2026 (6 days from today); (2) post-RFI evaluation and potential vendor workshops/demos ('we will request demos after receiving the RFI if we feel we need them'); (3) commercial route decision (CCS framework, G-Cloud, open market, or direct award if one supplier is significantly superior); (4) final vendor selection and contract award by end of 2026 or early 2027 to allow implementation before August 2027 Intent contract expiry. Sophie stated 'it may be that we could do anything from going through a framework where it's a competitive tender, to considering a direct award if one supplier comes out and shows that they are 1,000,000 times better and comes in with an extremely competitive price.' Leadership change at the council delayed the RFI release (Next Step: 'we have had a change in leadership which has delayed the project... it should pick up at pace now'). The evaluation committee will include procurement, IT (an architect), and contract management evaluators; formal committee membership not yet finalized. No approval chain beyond Lucy Moody (sponsor) and John Coyne (director) has been described.

sources: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks
SF Next Step: RFI primer support request around AI capabilities, Oracle integration, procurement volumes, framework preference, implementation model
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer's draft RFI (15 questions, pre-market engagement questionnaire) and Sophie's 22 Apr call statements provide the preliminary evaluation criteria: (1) full Source-to-Pay coverage (intake/triage, sourcing, contract management, supplier risk, spend visibility) replacing manual Excel/SharePoint processes; (2) Oracle ERP integration (mandatory); (3) AI capabilities for procurement/contract process improvement (specifically called out as a new RFI question — 'our requirements at the moment don't include AI, we're adding a question in there to see what kind of capabilities you've got'); (4) implementation timeline to meet August 2027 Intent contract expiry; (5) cost estimates and commercial model; (6) framework/commercial route compatibility (CCS, G-Cloud, or open market); (7) implementation model (vendor-led vs GSI partner such as Capgemini/KPMG/Deloitte). The RFI is designed to gather vendor responses for deeper evaluation; formal scoring criteria and weighting have not been shared. Confirmed: the evaluation committee will be 'procurement and IT... an architect from our side and various people from procurement contract management' (not yet named). Decision criteria will concretize post-RFI response (6 Jul deadline) and during vendor workshops.

ai_fit_signal.summary: Buyer is AI Curious trending toward AI Hungry. Sophie Onyeks stated on the 22 Apr call 'we're very interested in AI' and the council proactively added an AI capabilities question to the RFI after initial drafting, asking vendors 'what kind of capabilities you've got and what you could do to improve anything that we're doing at the moment.' This is appetite-driven curiosity (not compliance-driven or passive). The council has not articulated specific AI use cases yet, so they are in discovery mode, but their operational pain (manual contract management, no intake automation, no spend visibility dashboards) and £1.7 billion scale create a strong AI fit. If Zycus demonstrates concrete AI automation (Merlin intake triage, autonomous tail-spend negotiation, obligation tracking, risk dashboards) in the RFI response and workshops, the buyer could move from Curious to Hungry. No AIS score populated in Salesforce; this is a pre-qualification assessment.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI positioning is strong and well-timed. Birmingham City Council explicitly added an AI capabilities question to the draft RFI after initial drafting ('we're very interested in AI... we're adding a question in there to see what kind of capabilities you've got'), signaling genuine appetite. Sophie stated the council's current requirements 'don't include AI' but they want to understand what vendors can offer to 'improve anything that we're doing at the moment.' Anthony Gray (Zycus VP EMEA) presented Merlin agentic AI on the 22 Apr call (intake control tower, autonomous negotiation, auto-contract-read, spend analysis Q&A, sourcing event automation) and positioned Zycus as a 20-year AI-powered platform ('we've been doing AI powered procurement for over 20 years... agentic AI platform'). The buyer's primary pain (manual Excel/SharePoint contract management, no centralized intake, no spend visibility) directly maps to Merlin's automation capabilities. AI is a differentiator vs competitors if Zycus makes it the centerpiece of the RFI response and post-RFI workshops. No risk of over-positioning; the buyer is actively soliciting AI use cases.

items.quote: We want to get away from all of that and have a central system which basically does everything as well as managing risks and issues, managing procurement projects.
items.source: Avoma discovery call 22 Apr 2026, Sophie Onyeks
items.criterion: Full Source-to-Pay with deep contract management (obligations, renewals, risk dashboards, central repository)

items.quote: We've just applied for Crown Commercial Services, so that will be on as a Crown vendor... said June is when we'll be on there. Right now, we are engaged in a large project with the Scottish government.

items.criterion: Implementation model flexibility (vendor-led vs GSI partner)

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Birmingham City Council's requirements align closely with Zycus's platform strengths: full Source-to-Pay with deep contract management (the buyer's primary pain), Oracle ERP integration (Zycus has Oracle connectors), AI capabilities (Merlin agentic AI is a differentiator), intake/triage automation (Merlin control tower), and spend visibility/supplier risk dashboards (Zycus analytics). Two partial-alignment areas: (1) CCS framework access — Zycus applied, approval expected June 2026 (today is 30 Jun, status unclear; if not approved, GSI subcontract via Capgemini/KPMG/Deloitte on G-Cloud is fallback); (2) UK public-sector references — Anthony Gray stated UK gov/council sector 'opened up for us in the last 6 months', implying limited UK public-sector install base (though Scottish government project underway). No misalignment has surfaced; the buyer's criteria (full platform, Oracle integration, AI, implementation flexibility) match Zycus's offering. Risk: if a competitor (e.g. SAP Ariba, Jaggaer) has deeper UK council references and native CCS access, they could win on trust/ease despite Zycus's product edge.

hard.ais_why:
Buyer stated explicit AI curiosity: 'We're very interested in AI. That's definitely something that we want... some kind of idea from when we send out the RFI.'
But AI is not yet a requirement: 'our requirements at the moment... don't include AI. We're adding a question in there to see what... capabilities you've got.'
Single intro call; early pre-market stage with manual/Oracle current state. Medium confidence.

We will request demos after receiving the RFI if we feel we need demos at this stage. It is something we haven’t discussed in detail with the team yet.
